subsystem: Linux kernel RDS (Reliable Datagram Sockets) socket-option handler. rds_recv_track_latency() implements the setsockopt path that lets a userspace application enable per-datagram receive-latency tracing and choose which trace points to record. It copies a fixed-size control structure from user space, validates a caller-supplied count and a set of caller-supplied trace-point indices, and stores them into the per-socket rds_sock state (rs_rx_traces / rs_rx_trace[]) for use by the RDS receive path.
provenance: Identified from the RDS-specific symbols and Linux kernel idioms: the function name rds_recv_track_latency, the struct rds_sock *rs handle, the struct rds_rx_trace_so control structure, the RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX constant, and the per-socket fields rs->rs_rx_traces and rs->rs_rx_trace[]. The __user annotation on optval, the copy_from_user() primitive, and the -EFAULT return convention are Linux kernel uaccess conventions; this is net/rds/af_rds.c. The banner comment '/* sample: primevul_c_train_3060_fixed */' marks it as a PrimeVul-derived sample tagged as the FIXED variant.
trust_boundary: This is a setsockopt(2) handler, so the trust boundary is the syscall entry from an unprivileged userspace process. optval is a char __user * pointing at attacker-controlled memory and optlen is an attacker-controlled length. Everything read via copy_from_user into the local 'trace' object is hostile: trace.rx_traces (the element count) and every entry of trace.rx_trace_pos[] are fully attacker-chosen. The destination rs (the rds_sock) is trusted kernel state, but the values written into rs->rs_rx_traces and rs->rs_rx_trace[] flow directly from the untrusted struct, so the count and each index must be bounds-checked before use.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The function signature. rs is the trusted per-socket RDS state that will receive the parsed configuration. optval is a userspace pointer (char __user *) supplied through setsockopt; it is attacker-controlled memory that must be copied in with copy_from_user, never dereferenced directly. optlen is the userspace-provided option length as a signed int. The function returns an int status (0 on success, a negative errno on failure). Its job is to translate a user-provided rds_rx_trace_so option blob into the socket's latency-tracing configuration.

Local variables. 'trace' is an on-stack copy of the fixed-size control structure that the caller submits; per the RDS uAPI this structure contains an 8-bit count field (rx_traces) followed by a small fixed array (rx_trace_pos[RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX]) of trace-point selectors. 'i' is the loop induction variable used to iterate over the requested trace positions. Because 'trace' lives on the kernel stack, any over-read or over-write driven by its unvalidated count/index fields would corrupt kernel memory.

The length gate. It requires the userspace-declared optlen to exactly equal sizeof(struct rds_rx_trace_so). An exact-equality check (rather than a >= minimum) is the correct discipline for a fixed-layout control struct: it rejects both truncated blobs (which would leave part of 'trace' uninitialized after the copy) and oversized blobs. Because it is an equality test, a negative or absurdly large optlen also fails here, so the signedness of optlen is neutralized before it matters.

Bulk copy of the entire control structure from user space into the stack object 'trace'. copy_from_user validates that the source range is a legal user address and returns the number of uncopied bytes (nonzero on fault), which is treated as -EFAULT. After this line every field of 'trace' — the rx_traces count and all rx_trace_pos[] entries — is fully attacker-controlled data that has NOT yet been range-checked. The copy length is sizeof(trace), consistent with the optlen gate above.

The count bound check — the security-critical guard of this routine. trace.rx_traces is the attacker-chosen number of trace positions to record. It is compared against RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX, the compile-time dimension of both the source array trace.rx_trace_pos[] and the destination array rs->rs_rx_trace[]. Rejecting any count strictly greater than RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX is what prevents the subsequent loop from writing past the fixed-size rs->rs_rx_trace[] buffer. This check is precisely the fix that distinguishes this 'fixed' sample from the vulnerable original, in which an unchecked rx_traces (an 8-bit value up to 255) drove the store loop out of bounds.

Commits the validated count into the socket. Because line 14 already bounded trace.rx_traces to at most RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX, rs->rs_rx_traces now holds a value in [0, RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX]. Note the count is written to the persistent socket state before the per-element index validation runs; the error path below is responsible for undoing this if any index is invalid.

The store loop. It iterates i from 0 up to rs->rs_rx_traces-1, i.e. at most RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX-1, so both the read trace.rx_trace_pos[i] and the write rs->rs_rx_trace[i] index within the fixed arrays given the line-14 bound. For each entry it validates the trace-point selector value against RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX; if an entry is out of range it zeroes rs->rs_rx_traces (so a later reader does not act on a half-configured, stale array) and returns -EFAULT. Valid entries are copied into rs->rs_rx_trace[]. These stored values are later consumed by the RDS receive path as indices to select which latency measurement points to record.

Success return after the full count and every requested trace position have been validated and stored. On this path rs->rs_rx_traces and rs->rs_rx_trace[0..rs_rx_traces-1] hold a fully validated configuration.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This is the central attack surface and the exact site of the historical bug (CWE-787 out-of-bounds write). The write rs->rs_rx_trace[i] is bounded ONLY by the loop limit rs->rs_rx_traces, which is copied from the attacker-controlled 8-bit trace.rx_traces (range 0..255). The destination rs->rs_rx_trace[] is a fixed array of RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX elements. If the guard at line 14 were absent or wrong, an attacker setting rx_traces up to 255 would drive i past the array bound and overwrite adjacent rds_sock fields on the kernel heap — a classic bounded-buffer-vs-count overflow. In this fixed sample the line-14 check neutralizes it, so the residual risk is a CONTRACT/LATENT one: correctness depends entirely on the unseen header guaranteeing that RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX is exactly the dimension of BOTH trace.rx_trace_pos[] and rs->rs_rx_trace[]. If either array were ever declared smaller than RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX, the '>' comparison would no longer protect the store.

Off-by-one hazard on the trace-point VALUE check. The stored values rs->rs_rx_trace[i] are consumed by the RDS receive path as indices into a per-message latency array (i_rx_lat_trace[]) that is itself dimensioned RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX. This check uses strict '>' , so a selector value exactly equal to RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX is accepted and stored. For a zero-based array of RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX elements the last valid index is RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX-1, so if the enumeration of valid trace points runs 0..RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX-1 the check should be '>=' and this is a latent one-past-the-end out-of-bounds access (write or read, CWE-787/CWE-125) in the consumer. Whether this is truly a bug depends on the semantics of RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX (marker-past-the-end count vs. a valid trailing index) in the header that is not shown; it must be confirmed against the recv-path indexing code.

Ordering / partial-update concern. rs->rs_rx_traces is committed to the shared socket state at line 17 before every element has been validated, and rs->rs_rx_trace[0..i-1] are filled incrementally. If a concurrent RDS receive on the same socket could observe rs->rs_rx_traces and iterate rs->rs_rx_trace[] between line 17 and the loop's completion, it might read entries not yet written on this call (stale contents from a previous configuration). The error path zeroes rs->rs_rx_traces before returning, which limits the window, and setsockopt is normally serialized by the socket lock, but the implementation must actually hold that lock (e.g. lock_sock) for the read side to be safe; confirm the locking discipline around rs_rx_traces/rs_rx_trace[].

The input-length gate itself is correct and important: the exact-equality test forces optlen to match the full structure, so 'trace' is fully overwritten by copy_from_user and no stack bytes are left uninitialized, and negative/huge optlen values are rejected here. Two minor observations: (1) the error code -EFAULT is a semantic mismatch for a bad length — -EINVAL is the conventional errno — but this is a hygiene issue, not a memory-safety one; (2) the safety of the copy assumes struct rds_rx_trace_so has no attacker-relevant internal padding whose uninitialized bytes could later be exposed, which holds for the u8 count + u8[] layout but should be confirmed against the uAPI definition.

Data-trust reminder: after copy_from_user every field of 'trace' is untrusted, and nothing about the source pointer or the byte contents is validated beyond the two range checks. The design is safe only because those two checks (count at line 14, per-element value at line 19) are the sole gate between attacker bytes and fixed-size kernel arrays. There is no cryptographic/magic validation to bypass here, so the entire security of the routine rests on those numeric bounds being correct relative to the array dimensions — which is why the exact value of RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX and the array declarations are the things to verify.

summary: This is the FIXED variant of the RDS setsockopt latency-tracing handler. The historical out-of-bounds write (CWE-787) — an unchecked 8-bit rx_traces count driving a store loop past the fixed rs->rs_rx_trace[] array — is correctly closed by the count bound check at line 14, and the store/read indices are consequently in range. The residual risks are latent and depend on the unseen header: the guards use strict '>' against RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX, so the correctness of both the count bound and the per-element value bound (a possible off-by-one for a zero-based, MAX-sized consumer array) hinges on RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX being exactly the array dimension. No exploitable overflow is present in the shown code.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: rds_recv_track_latency(struct rds_sock *rs, char __user *optval, int optlen) (signature, lines 2-3)
reviewed_anchors.reason: setsockopt entry point; optval/optlen are the untrusted syscall inputs that seed the whole routine.
On-stack copy of the attacker-controlled control structure; its count and index fields drive all subsequent array accesses.
Loop induction variable bounded by rs->rs_rx_traces; carries no independent security contract.
Exact-length gate; correct but uses -EFAULT instead of -EINVAL and underpins the fully-initialized copy — minor/info.
Copy length matches the length-gated struct size and fault is handled; establishes that all trace fields are untrusted but is itself correct.
The count-vs-fixed-buffer guard; its correctness (and the '>' boundary vs. array dimension) is the crux preventing CWE-787.
Commits the count to shared socket state before per-element validation; partial-update / ordering contract.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: for (i = 0; i < rs->rs_rx_traces; i++) ... rs->rs_rx_trace[i] = trace.rx_trace_pos[i] (lines 18-24)
reviewed_anchors.reason: The store loop and fixed-buffer write site; historical OOB write location, now bounded by line 14.
Per-element value check with strict '>' — potential off-by-one for a zero-based MAX-sized consumer array.
The single constant that must equal both array dimensions; the entire memory-safety argument rests on its value, which is defined in a header not shown.
Fixed-size destination buffer paired with the attacker-supplied rx_traces count; per method this pairing must get a finding.
Read within [0, rs_rx_traces) which line 14 bounds to <= RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX; in-bounds given the same array dimension, no separate over-read.
Error propagation; -EFAULT for a bad length/value is a conventions nit, not a memory-safety issue.

findings.title: Attacker-controlled count drives fixed-size store loop (bounded by the guard, contract-critical)
findings.anchor: for (i = 0; i < rs->rs_rx_traces; i++) { ... rs->rs_rx_trace[i] = trace.rx_trace_pos[i]; }
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds write (fixed buffer driven by attacker count)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0291_cwe787.c (this file); the array declarations live in the header/struct definitions (files not provided)
findings.invariant: The loop bound rs->rs_rx_traces must never exceed the element count of rs->rs_rx_trace[], and RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX (used at line 14) must equal that array dimension exactly, so that i and thus rs->rs_rx_trace[i] stay in range.
findings.failure_mode: trace.rx_traces is an 8-bit attacker value (0..255). Without the line-14 check, setting it large makes i run far past rs->rs_rx_trace[RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX], overwriting adjacent rds_sock heap memory — the original CVE-class OOB write. In this fixed sample the check bounds it; the finding is retained because the safety is entirely contingent on the unseen array being dimensioned to RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX.
findings.what_to_confirm: In the RDS header, confirm that rs->rs_rx_trace[] and struct rds_rx_trace_so.rx_trace_pos[] are both declared with dimension RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX, and that no code path reaches this loop with rs->rs_rx_traces > that dimension.

findings.title: Off-by-one risk in trace-position value check ('>' vs '>=')
findings.bug_class: Off-by-one / out-of-bounds index in downstream consumer
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0291_cwe787.c (validation); the index is dereferenced in the RDS receive path (implementation file not provided)
findings.invariant: Every stored rs->rs_rx_trace[i] must be a valid index into the per-message latency array indexed at receive time; for a zero-based array of RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX elements each value must be strictly less than RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX.
findings.failure_mode: Because the comparison is strict '>', a selector value exactly equal to RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX passes and is stored. If the recv path uses that value to index a MAX-sized latency array (i_rx_lat_trace[]), reading/writing element [RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX] is one past the end. Whether this triggers depends on whether RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX is a past-the-end count or a valid trailing index in the enum, which is not shown here.
findings.what_to_confirm: Check the enum of trace points and the dimension of the latency array indexed by rs->rs_rx_trace[] in the recv path; determine whether the maximum valid selector is RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX-1 (making this an OOB) or RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX (making '>' correct).

findings.title: Count committed to shared socket state before per-element validation
findings.bug_class: Partial update of shared state / potential stale-read race
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0291_cwe787.c; the concurrent reader is the RDS recv path (file not provided)
findings.invariant: No other thread may observe rs->rs_rx_traces > 0 together with rs->rs_rx_trace[] entries that have not yet been written on the current call; setsockopt must be serialized against the receive path that reads these fields.
findings.failure_mode: rs->rs_rx_traces is set at line 17 and rs->rs_rx_trace[] is filled incrementally; if a concurrent receive on the same socket reads these without shared locking, it could act on a half-populated array (stale prior contents). The error path zeroes rs->rs_rx_traces to bound the window, but correctness relies on the socket lock covering both writer and reader.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify the caller holds lock_sock (or equivalent) across this function and that the recv path reads rs_rx_traces/rs_rx_trace[] under the same lock.

findings.title: Length-mismatch and value-range errors return -EFAULT instead of -EINVAL
findings.invariant: Errors should map to the conventional errno so userspace can distinguish a bad-argument (EINVAL) from a bad-address (EFAULT) failure; the length gate itself must reject any optlen not exactly equal to the struct size.
findings.failure_mode: A malformed optlen or an out-of-range trace value returns -EFAULT, which conventionally signals a bad user pointer; the length/value validity failures should be -EINVAL. This is not a memory-safety issue — the exact-equality length check is correct and prevents partial copies — but it is a semantic/hygiene defect and slightly weakens diagnosability.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the intended errno convention for this setsockopt and that the exact-length check is the only path allowing copy_from_user to run.

audit_checklist: Confirm RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX equals the declared dimension of BOTH struct rds_rx_trace_so.rx_trace_pos[] and rds_sock.rs_rx_trace[]; the entire bounds argument rests on this.
Determine whether valid trace-point selectors are 0..RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX-1 (making the '>' at line 19 an off-by-one that should be '>=') or 0..RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX.
Inspect the RDS receive path that consumes rs->rs_rx_trace[] as indices into i_rx_lat_trace[] and confirm those indices are in range for every stored value.
Verify the count bound at line 14 uses the correct comparison so rs->rs_rx_traces can never exceed the store array dimension, including the boundary case rx_traces == RDS_MSG_RX_DGRAM_TRACE_MAX.
Confirm setsockopt serialization (lock_sock or equivalent) covers both the write of rs_rx_traces/rs_rx_trace[] here and any concurrent reader in the recv path.
Check that struct rds_rx_trace_so has no uninitialized padding exposed after copy_from_user, and that the exact-length optlen gate cannot be bypassed.
Consider replacing -EFAULT with -EINVAL for the length/value validation failures to match kernel errno conventions.
